title: Locality-Induced Hierarchical Backflow Wavefunctions for Correlated Fermions
authors: Yu-Tong Zhou, Zheng-Wei Zhou, Wen-Yuan Liu
abstract: We show that locality provides a natural principle to hierarchically organize backflow wavefunctions. This leads us to propose a family of variational fermionic states, termed hierarchical backflow (HB) wavefunctions. The expressive power of HB is systematically improvable, controlled by a path depth $K$ which reflects the range of backflow correlations. At half-filling, the HB with $K=1$ already achieves high energy precision, with an accuracy around $0.5\%$ for system sizes from $4\times 4$ to $10\times 10$. At hole doping $n_h=0.125$, the method scales efficiently to $12\times16$ and $16\times16$ systems, and the energy systematically achieves higher accuracy with $K$ increasing, yielding a clear stripe phase. The HB further enables a local-nonlocal decomposition, naturally bridging to neural quantum states, while featuring compact representations and efficient optimization. Our work reveals locality as a natural organizing principle of backflow wavefunctions, opening a new framework with systematic improvability and interpretability for large-scale simulations of correlated fermion systems.
summary: This work introduces the Hierarchical Backflow (HB) wavefunction, a novel, systematically improvable ansatz for correlated fermions, by leveraging the principle of locality. By extending this to the Residual HB (RHB) ansatz, the authors achieve high accuracy in simulating the Hubbard model, demonstrating a powerful, scalable framework for strongly correlated electron physics.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of simulating and representing the ground state of strongly correlated fermionic systems, such as those described by the Hubbard model, due to the exponentially large Hilbert space.
main_result: The proposed Hierarchical Backflow (HB) and Residual Hierarchical Backflow (RHB) wavefunctions provide a systematically improvable and highly accurate variational ansatz, achieving results comparable to advanced quantum Monte Carlo methods.
method: The core method involves constructing a variational wavefunction based on the backflow concept, which is systematically refined by introducing a path depth parameter K, and further enhanced by a nonlocal neural network factor (RHB).
model_or_system: The study focuses on the two-dimensional Hubbard model, characterized by hopping ($t_{ij}$) and on-site repulsion ($U_{ik}$), applied to various doping levels and system sizes.
key_observables: Ground-state energy per site, staggered spin correlation function, and local spin/hole density distributions.
important_parameters: Path depth K (controlling correlation range), doping level ($n_h$), and interaction strength ($U$).
main_assumptions: The method relies on the locality principle to organize backflow correlations, and the RHB ansatz uses a two-stage optimization scheme to overcome trapping in the optimization landscape.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the orbital structure dependence on K, energy convergence curves showing systematic improvement with K, and spatial maps revealing the formation of stripe phases at finite doping.
paper_structure: The paper develops the HB ansatz by proposing locality as the organizing principle, systematically improving its expressive power via K. It then extends this to the RHB ansatz, which incorporates nonlocal correlations via a neural network, and validates this structure using advanced optimization techniques and comparisons to established benchmarks.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter, the development of a structured, systematically improvable ansatz (HB/RHB) that bridges determinantal forms with neural network representations is highly relevant for developing general-purpose quantum state representations applicable across different physical domains.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Orbital structure of hierarchical backﬂow (HB) wavefunc- tions at a given site i for diﬀerent path depths K, illustrating how K controls the range of backﬂow correlations. (a) K = 0: the orbital de- pends only on site i, corresponding to the Hartree-Fock state with no backﬂow correlations. (b) K = 1: the orbital at site i depends on its nearby sites j. (c) K = 2: the orbital further includes sites p that are neighbors of j; increasing K systematically enhances the expressive power. (d) Although each orbital depends only on local paths (here shown for K = 2), the Slater determinant couples them to generate many-body correlations in the wavefunction amplitude W(s).

FIG. 2. Energies of the two-dimensional Hubbard model under PBC with U = 8 as a function of the path depth K (K = 0, 1, 2) at (a) half-ﬁlling and (b) nh = 0.125 hole doping. K = 0 corresponds to the Hartree-Fock state. Increasing K systematically lowers the energy. (c) Comparison of staggered spin correlation functions C(r) = (−1)x+y⟨S(0, 0) · S(x, y)⟩as a function of distance r = p
FIG. 4. Energy comparison with NNB [31] and HFDS [32] for dif- ferent sizes at nh = 0.125 and U = 8, including HB K = 1, K = 2 and the RHB.

FIG. 3. The distribution of hole density (color map) and local spin S z moment (arrow length proportional to magnitude) on (a) 12 × 16 and (b) 16 × 16 PBC lattice at nh = 0.125 hole doping with U = 8.

FIG. 5. Conventional neural network states employ a global archi- tecture (left), whereas our physics-grounded ansatz features a local- nonlocal structure (right). For the same number of determinants, which provides similar expressive power, the number of variational parameters in our representation scales as O(NneuronN) + O(N2), fa- vorable compared to O(NneuronN2) for global architectures.
